question.text: A trader using currency derivatives for hedging wants to apply hedge accounting. What additional benefit does hedge accounting provide over regular mark-to-market accounting?

answer.text: Hedge accounting allows matching the timing of gains/losses on the hedging instrument with the hedged item, reducing P&L volatility from the hedging activity
Hedge accounting provides a lower tax rate on derivative gains
Hedge accounting eliminates the need to mark derivative positions to market value
Hedge accounting allows derivative losses to be carried forward for 12 years instead of 8

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Without hedge accounting, derivative MTM goes to P&L immediately while the hedged item (e.g., a forecast transaction) may not yet affect P&L. This mismatch creates P&L volatility despite an economic hedge. Hedge accounting aligns the timing — e.g., in a cash flow hedge, the effective hedge gain/loss goes to OCI until the hedged item affects P&L. **The Trap:** Hedge accounting doesn't change tax rates or carry-forward periods. Derivatives still need to be measured at fair value — it changes WHERE the gain/loss is recognized (P&L vs OCI), not whether. **Cross-Exam Context:** Hedge accounting is particularly relevant for currency hedging (I Ch9) and interest rate hedging (IV Ch9). Series XIII tests the cross-asset benefit. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Hedge accounting is mainly used by corporates, not traders. Traders typically don't qualify because their derivative positions don't meet the formal hedging relationship criteria.
